corrected_thesis: The 'cognitive persistence mechanics' stack. Anchoring sets an initial reference point that frames all subsequent judgment; cognitive-load-theory explains why the brain offloads to automatic processing; checklists externalize procedural knowledge to free working memory; habit-formation converts effortful behavior into automatic response; lock-in creates commitment persistence that resists re-evaluation; switching-costs impose friction against departing the incumbent state. This stack maps the full lifecycle of cognitive stickiness: an idea, process, or commitment enters through anchoring or checklist adoption, becomes automatic through habit, and resists displacement through lock-in and switching costs. CLT is the unifying explanatory mechanism — every other member either reduces cognitive load (checklists, habits) or exploits it (anchoring, lock-in, switching costs).
what_this_stack_defeats: Ignoring persistence effects, assuming rational switching, underestimating the cost of behavior change
notes: Auto-named cluster — no pre-existing thesis in FAMILY_DEFINITIONS. This is not a reasoning stack but a descriptive mechanics stack: it maps how cognitive commitments form and persist. Dual use: defensively (recognize when you're locked in) and offensively (design systems that stick). Canonical articles confirm the shared mechanism: anchoring article explicitly discusses scaffolding and cognitive load; checklists article calls them 'external cognitive scaffolds' that reduce CLT load; habit-formation article describes the cue-craving-response-reward loop as System 1 automation; lock-in and switching-costs articles both ground persistence in commitment-and-consistency bias.
